title: What happens when the information runs out
excerpt: When I asked AI to colorize old family photographs, it gave my brother eyebrows that aren't on the negative. That's when I started thinking less about color and more about information.
content_html:
My brother is eight years old in this photograph. He’s kneeling in our front yard in South Bend, late in the summer of 1976, squinting into the sun. His jeans have tan patches on the knees. His tan tank top has brown piping around the neck and arms. I know these things because I was there, and after 50 years I somehow still remember.

I was experimenting with AI colorization of some black-and-white scans, curious what the technology could actually do.

I told it the colors I remembered. The cedar shingles were dull green. The gable was white. His jeans were blue with those tan patches. ChatGPT took all of that readily enough. The house’s green isn’t exactly right, but it’s close. There’s a neighbor’s car in the background that I remember but only vaguely — ChatGPT coloring it pale blue works well enough.

Then I looked at my brother’s face. ChatGPT had given him eyebrows — and got them wrong. That’s not my brother anymore. It’s an impostor.

You can’t see his eyebrows on the b/w scan. I shot that negative with a simple camera on drug-store film, and digitized it with one of those “digitizers” you can buy on Amazon for a hundred bucks. Maybe it just didn’t pull out all of the information from the negative.

Then I remembered I’d also scanned the same negative on an Epson V300 flatbed with SilverFast, a much better setup. It brought out more detail and richer tonality. Yet even this didn’t reveal my brother’s eyebrows. The eyebrows were never on the negative to begin with.

The AI had no way to know that. It saw a face with missing information and filled the gap.

I also asked ChatGPT to colorize a print scan I made a long time ago of this road crew building what would become US 40 in east-central Illinois in the early 1920s. This was a small print of maybe three inches on the long side. The Canon CanoScan 9000F scanner I used is quite capable, but it can resolve only so much information from such a print.

I had an ace up my sleeve this time — I have actual photographs of these bricks as they looked in 2007 as an abandoned road. I shared them with ChatGPT and asked it to colorize using the photos as a reference.

ChatGPT did well with the bricks (but to be fair, even then it had to guess at which bricks were which color). The tar under the bricks went black. It wasn’t a stretch for ChatGPT to guess that eastern Illinois grass is green and dirt is brown. The workers got faded blues and grays and tans that looked right for the era, at least based on the modern movies set in that era that I’ve watched. But there’s no way to know what color any of their clothes were that day.

Then I noticed the sign. ChatGPT had tried to resolve it into information. “USA Liberty Loan” it says now. But when I look at the original scan, the middle line reads more like “Lockerbie” to me, and the lines above and below simply aren’t legible.

That’s when I started thinking less about color and more about information. Working through these images, I kept sorting what I had into three piles.

  • Some information survives, is known, and can be described — the bricks are still there, I can walk on them and photograph them.
  • Some information can be reasonably inferred — workwear colors in the 1920s have a limited color range; a car from the 1970s could plausibly be pale blue, and a reasonable guess is a reasonable guess.
  • Some information is simply lost, or was never present to begin with, and this is where AI will make choices that usually don’t track.

My brother’s eyebrows and the lettering on that sign are in the last category — at least based on the source material available. If the road-crew negative survives somewhere, maybe it could be drum scanned to draw out every pixel of available information, and maybe then the sign would become legible. Maybe. I feel certain that drum scanning the negative of my brother wouldn’t magically make his eyebrows appear, given the very real limitations of the lens and film that made the photo.

I went back and explicitly told ChatGPT not to resolve details that didn’t exist in the file. It still did anyway, just to a far lesser extent. It removed slats from the gable and sharpened a lot of details. But my brother’s face was much better. You can sense eyebrows without seeing them. The face tracked true.

I didn’t know I needed to tell AI not to invent eyebrows until I saw it invent eyebrows. From that I began to learn how this technology works: gaps are problems to be solved, and AI’s default is to solve them. The AI won’t leave them alone unless you tell it to, and even then it still sometimes can’t help itself.

After I got over being irritated, I realized that nobody walked into a darkroom and made a perfect print on the first sheet. You made a print, looked hard at it, and noticed what bothered you. Re-print with more or less light. Burn the sky. Dodge the face. Try again. The iteration was the process, not a detour around it. I wrote about that here.

After my mother died, some hand-colored family photographs came to me. Someone skilled did them. But the artist made choices that aren’t quite right on people I knew. Hair a little too golden. An eyebrow slightly too arched. The colorist was working from the same information I give ChatGPT: the photograph and whatever the client told them. The artist made his best inferences. Some worked and some didn’t.

AI restoration works the same way, which shouldn’t be surprising. What surprised me was realizing that the human’s job in this process isn’t to supervise the AI. It’s to communicate certainty clearly — but also to put uncertainty back in. To know what the colors were when you know them, yes, but also to know when you don’t know. To recognize the difference between a gap that might plausibly be filled and a gap that must remain permanent.

The AI sees pixels. I know things that are and aren’t in the scan. I know what color the house was. I know what those bricks look like today. I know my brother’s face well enough to know when it’s wrong.

That last one might be the more important skill.

blocks.html_en: My brother is eight years old in this photograph. He’s kneeling in our front yard in South Bend, late in the summer of 1976, squinting into the sun. His jeans have tan patches on the knees. His tan tank top has brown piping around the neck and arms. I know these things because I was there, and after 50 years I somehow still remember.

sentences.en: My brother is eight years old in this photograph.
sentences.zh: 这张照片中我弟弟八岁。

sentences.en: He’s kneeling in our front yard in South Bend, late in the summer of 1976, squinting into the sun.
sentences.zh: 1976 年夏末，他跪在我们位于南本德的前院，眯着眼睛看着阳光。

sentences.en: His jeans have tan patches on the knees.
sentences.zh: 他的牛仔裤膝盖上有棕褐色补丁。

sentences.en: His tan tank top has brown piping around the neck and arms.
sentences.zh: 他的棕褐色背心的脖子和手臂上有棕色滚边。

sentences.en: I know these things because I was there, and after 50 years I somehow still remember.
sentences.zh: 我知道这些事情是因为我在那里，而且 50 年后我仍然记得。

blocks.html_en: I was experimenting with AI colorization of some black-and-white scans, curious what the technology could actually do.

sentences.en: I was experimenting with AI colorization of some black-and-white scans, curious what the technology could actually do.
sentences.zh: 我正在尝试对一些黑白扫描件进行人工智能着色，很好奇这项技术实际上能做什么。

blocks.html_en: I told it the colors I remembered. The cedar shingles were dull green. The gable was white. His jeans were blue with those tan patches. ChatGPT took all of that readily enough. sentences.en: I told it the colors I remembered.
sentences.zh: 我告诉它我记得的颜色。

sentences.en: The cedar shingles were dull green.
sentences.zh: 雪松瓦呈暗绿色。

sentences.en: The gable was white.
sentences.zh: 山墙是白色的。

sentences.en: His jeans were blue with those tan patches.
sentences.zh: 他的牛仔裤是蓝色的，上面有棕褐色的补丁。

sentences.en: ChatGPT took all of that readily enough.
sentences.zh: ChatGPT 很容易地接受了这一切。

sentences.en: The house’s green isn’t exactly right, but it’s close.
sentences.zh: 房子的绿色并不完全正确，但也很接近了。

sentences.en: There’s a neighbor’s car in the background that I remember but only vaguely — ChatGPT coloring it pale blue works well enough.
sentences.zh: 我记得背景中有一辆邻居的车，但只是模糊地记得——ChatGPT 将其涂成淡蓝色效果很好。

blocks.html_en: Then I looked at my brother’s face. ChatGPT had given him eyebrows — and got them wrong. That’s not my brother anymore. It’s an impostor.

sentences.en: Then I looked at my brother’s face.
sentences.zh: 然后我看着哥哥的脸。

sentences.en: ChatGPT had given him eyebrows — and got them wrong.
sentences.zh: ChatGPT 给了他眉毛——但弄错了。

sentences.en: That’s not my brother anymore.
sentences.zh: 那不再是我的兄弟了。

sentences.en: It’s an impostor.
sentences.zh: 这是一个骗子。

sentences.zh: 我用一台简单的相机在药店胶片上拍摄了这张底片，然后用你可以在亚马逊上花一百美元买到的“数字化仪”将其数字化。

sentences.en: Maybe it just didn’t pull out all of the information from the negative.
sentences.zh: 也许它只是没有从底片中提取出所有信息。

sentences.en: It brought out more detail and richer tonality.
sentences.zh: 它带来了更多细节和更丰富的色调。

sentences.en: Yet even this didn’t reveal my brother’s eyebrows.
sentences.zh: 但即便如此，我哥哥的眉毛也没有露出来。

sentences.en: The eyebrows were never on the negative to begin with.
sentences.zh: 眉毛从一开始就不是负数。

blocks.html_en: The AI had no way to know that. It saw a face with missing information and filled the gap.

sentences.en: The AI had no way to know that.
sentences.zh: 人工智能无法知道这一点。

sentences.en: It saw a face with missing information and filled the gap.
sentences.zh: 它看到了一张缺少信息的面孔并填补了空白。

sentences.en: I also asked ChatGPT to colorize a print scan I made a long time ago of this road crew building what would become US 40 in east-central Illinois in the early 1920s.
sentences.zh: 我还要求 ChatGPT 对我很久以前制作的打印扫描件进行着色，该扫描件是 1920 年代初在伊利诺伊州中东部建造的公路工作人员建造的，后来成为 US 40。

sentences.en: This was a small print of maybe three inches on the long side.
sentences.zh: 这是一个长边大约三英寸的小印刷品。

sentences.en: The Canon CanoScan 9000F scanner I used is quite capable, but it can resolve only so much information from such a print.
sentences.zh: 我使用的佳能 CanoScan 9000F 扫描仪功能相当强大，但它只能从此类打印中解析有限的信息。

sentences.en: I had an ace up my sleeve this time — I have actual photographs of these bricks as they looked in 2007 as an abandoned road.
sentences.zh: 这次我有一张王牌——我有这些砖块的真实照片，它们在 2007 年看起来就像是一条废弃的道路。

sentences.en: I shared them with ChatGPT and asked it to colorize using the photos as a reference.
sentences.zh: 我与 ChatGPT 分享了它们，并要求它使用照片作为参考进行着色。

blocks.html_en: ChatGPT did well with the bricks (but to be fair, even then it had to guess at which bricks were which color). The tar under the bricks went black. The workers got faded blues and grays and tans that looked right for the era, at least based on the modern movies set in that era that I’ve watched. sentences.en: ChatGPT did well with the bricks (but to be fair, even then it had to guess at which bricks were which color).
sentences.zh: ChatGPT 在砖块方面表现良好（但公平地说，即使如此，它也必须猜测哪些砖块是什么颜色）。

sentences.en: The tar under the bricks went black.
sentences.zh: 砖块下面的焦油变黑了。

sentences.en: It wasn’t a stretch for ChatGPT to guess that eastern Illinois grass is green and dirt is brown.
sentences.zh: ChatGPT 毫不夸张地说，伊利诺伊州东部的草是绿色的，泥土是棕色的。

sentences.en: The workers got faded blues and grays and tans that looked right for the era, at least based on the modern movies set in that era that I’ve watched.
sentences.zh: 工人们的衣服是褪色的蓝色、灰色和棕褐色，看起来很适合那个时代，至少从我看过的那个时代背景的现代电影来看是这样。

sentences.en: But there’s no way to know what color any of their clothes were that day.
sentences.zh: 但无法知道他们那天的衣服是什么颜色。

blocks.html_en: Then I noticed the sign. ChatGPT had tried to resolve it into information. “USA Liberty Loan” it says now. But when I look at the original scan, the middle line reads more like “Lockerbie” to me, and the lines above and below simply aren’t legible.

sentences.en: Then I noticed the sign.
sentences.zh: 然后我注意到了这个标志。

sentences.en: ChatGPT had tried to resolve it into information.
sentences.zh: ChatGPT 试图将其解析为信息。

sentences.en: “USA Liberty Loan” it says now.
sentences.zh: 现在它说的是“美国自由贷款”。

sentences.en: But when I look at the original scan, the middle line reads more like “Lockerbie” to me, and the lines above and below simply aren’t legible.
sentences.zh: 但当我查看原始扫描件时，中间的一行对我来说更像是“洛克比”，而上面和下面的行根本无法辨认。

sentences.en: Maybe.
sentences.zh: 或许。

sentences.en: I feel certain that drum scanning the negative of my brother wouldn’t magically make his eyebrows appear, given the very real limitations of the lens and film that made the photo.
sentences.zh: 我确信，考虑到制作这张照片的镜头和胶片的真正局限性，鼓式扫描我兄弟的底片不会神奇地让他的眉毛出现。

sentences.zh: 无论如何，它仍然如此，只是程度要小得多。

sentences.en: It removed slats from the gable and sharpened a lot of details.
sentences.zh: 它拆除了山墙上的板条并锐化了许多细节。

sentences.en: But my brother’s face was much better.
sentences.zh: 不过哥哥的脸色好多了。

blocks.html_en: After I got over being irritated, I realized that nobody walked into a darkroom and made a perfect print on the first sheet. You made a print, looked hard at it, and noticed what bothered you. Re-print with more or less light. Burn the sky. Dodge the face. Try again. The iteration was the process, not a detour around it. I wrote about that here.

sentences.en: After I got over being irritated, I realized that nobody walked into a darkroom and made a perfect print on the first sheet.
sentences.zh: 当我克服了恼怒之后，我意识到没有人走进暗室并在第一张纸上打印出完美的照片。

sentences.en: You made a print, looked hard at it, and noticed what bothered you.
sentences.zh: 你打印了一张照片，仔细观察，发现了困扰你的事情。

sentences.en: Re-print with more or less light.
sentences.zh: 用或多或少的光线重新打印。

sentences.en: Burn the sky.
sentences.zh: 烧毁天空。

sentences.en: Dodge the face.
sentences.zh: 躲开脸。

sentences.en: Try again.
sentences.zh: 再试一次。

sentences.en: The iteration was the process, not a detour around it.
sentences.zh: 迭代是一个过程，而不是绕道而行。

sentences.en: I wrote about that here.
sentences.zh: 我在这里写过这一点。

blocks.html_en: After my mother died, some hand-colored family photographs came to me. Someone skilled did them. But the artist made choices that aren’t quite right on people I knew. Hair a little too golden. An eyebrow slightly too arched. The colorist was working from the same information I give ChatGPT: the photograph and whatever the client told them. The artist made his best inferences. Some worked and some didn’t.

sentences.en: After my mother died, some hand-colored family photographs came to me.
sentences.zh: 我母亲去世后，我收到了一些手绘的家庭照片。

sentences.en: Someone skilled did them.
sentences.zh: 有人熟练地做到了。

sentences.en: But the artist made choices that aren’t quite right on people I knew.
sentences.zh: 但这位艺术家做出的选择对我认识的人来说不太正确。

sentences.en: Hair a little too golden.
sentences.zh: 头发有点太金黄了。

sentences.en: An eyebrow slightly too arched.
sentences.zh: 眉毛有点太拱了。

sentences.en: The colorist was working from the same information I give ChatGPT: the photograph and whatever the client told them.
sentences.zh: 调色师根据我向 ChatGPT 提供的相同信息进行工作：照片以及客户告诉他们的任何信息。

sentences.en: The artist made his best inferences.
sentences.zh: 艺术家做出了他最好的推论。

sentences.en: Some worked and some didn’t.
sentences.zh: 有些有效，有些无效。

blocks.html_en: AI restoration works the same way, which shouldn’t be surprising. What surprised me was realizing that the human’s job in this process isn’t to supervise the AI. It’s to communicate certainty clearly — but also to put uncertainty back in. To know what the colors were when you know them, yes, but also to know when you don’t know. To recognize the difference between a gap that might plausibly be filled and a gap that must remain permanent.

sentences.en: AI restoration works the same way, which shouldn’t be surprising.
sentences.zh: 人工智能恢复的工作原理相同，这并不奇怪。

sentences.en: What surprised me was realizing that the human’s job in this process isn’t to supervise the AI.
sentences.zh: 令我惊讶的是，人类在这个过程中的工作并不是监督人工智能。

sentences.en: It’s to communicate certainty clearly — but also to put uncertainty back in.
sentences.zh: 这是为了清楚地传达确定性，同时也将不确定性重新带入。

sentences.en: To know what the colors were when you know them, yes, but also to know when you don’t know.
sentences.zh: 当你知道颜色时就知道它们是什么，是的，但当你不知道时也知道它们是什么。

sentences.en: To recognize the difference between a gap that might plausibly be filled and a gap that must remain permanent.
sentences.zh: 认识到可能被填补的差距和必须保持永久的差距之间的区别。

blocks.html_en: The AI sees pixels. I know things that are and aren’t in the scan. I know what color the house was. I know what those bricks look like today. I know my brother’s face well enough to know when it’s wrong.

sentences.en: The AI sees pixels.
sentences.zh: 人工智能看到的是像素。

sentences.en: I know things that are and aren’t in the scan.
sentences.zh: 我知道扫描中包含和未包含的内容。

sentences.en: I know what color the house was.
sentences.zh: 我知道房子是什么颜色的。

sentences.en: I know what those bricks look like today.
sentences.zh: 我知道那些砖块今天是什么样子。

sentences.en: I know my brother’s face well enough to know when it’s wrong.
sentences.zh: 我很熟悉我哥哥的脸，知道什么时候是错的。

blocks.html_en: That last one might be the more important skill.

sentences.en: That last one might be the more important skill.
sentences.zh: 最后一项可能是更重要的技能。

blocks.html_en: Get more of my photography in your inbox or reader! Click here to subscribe.

sentences.en: Get more of my photography in your inbox or reader!
sentences.zh: 在您的收件箱或阅读器中获取更多我的摄影作品！

sentences.en: Click here to subscribe.
sentences.zh: 单击此处订阅。

content_html_zh:
这张照片中我弟弟八岁。 1976 年夏末，他跪在我们位于南本德的前院，眯着眼睛看着阳光。 他的牛仔裤膝盖上有棕褐色补丁。 他的棕褐色背心的脖子和手臂上有棕色滚边。 我知道这些事情是因为我在那里，而且 50 年后我仍然记得。

我正在尝试对一些黑白扫描件进行人工智能着色，很好奇这项技术实际上能做什么。

我告诉它我记得的颜色。 雪松瓦呈暗绿色。 山墙是白色的。 他的牛仔裤是蓝色的，上面有棕褐色的补丁。 ChatGPT 很容易地接受了这一切。 房子的绿色并不完全正确，但也很接近了。 我记得背景中有一辆邻居的车，但只是模糊地记得——ChatGPT 将其涂成淡蓝色效果很好。

然后我看着哥哥的脸。 ChatGPT 给了他眉毛——但弄错了。 那不再是我的兄弟了。 这是一个骗子。

在黑白扫描中你看不到他的眉毛。 我用一台简单的相机在药店胶片上拍摄了这张底片，然后用你可以在亚马逊上花一百美元买到的“数字化仪”将其数字化。 也许它只是没有从底片中提取出所有信息。

然后我记得我还在 Epson V300 平板上使用 SilverFast 扫描了相同的底片，这是一个更好的设置。 它带来了更多细节和更丰富的色调。 但即便如此，我哥哥的眉毛也没有露出来。 眉毛从一开始就不是负数。

人工智能无法知道这一点。 它看到了一张缺少信息的面孔并填补了空白。

我还要求 ChatGPT 对我很久以前制作的打印扫描件进行着色，该扫描件是 1920 年代初在伊利诺伊州中东部建造的公路工作人员建造的，后来成为 US 40。 这是一个长边大约三英寸的小印刷品。 我使用的佳能 CanoScan 9000F 扫描仪功能相当强大，但它只能从此类打印中解析有限的信息。

这次我有一张王牌——我有这些砖块的真实照片，它们在 2007 年看起来就像是一条废弃的道路。 我与 ChatGPT 分享了它们，并要求它使用照片作为参考进行着色。

ChatGPT 在砖块方面表现良好（但公平地说，即使如此，它也必须猜测哪些砖块是什么颜色）。 砖块下面的焦油变黑了。 ChatGPT 毫不夸张地说，伊利诺伊州东部的草是绿色的，泥土是棕色的。 工人们的衣服是褪色的蓝色、灰色和棕褐色，看起来很适合那个时代，至少从我看过的那个时代背景的现代电影来看是这样。 但无法知道他们那天的衣服是什么颜色。

然后我注意到了这个标志。 ChatGPT 试图将其解析为信息。 现在它说的是“美国自由贷款”。 但当我查看原始扫描件时，中间的一行对我来说更像是“洛克比”，而上面和下面的行根本无法辨认。

从那时起，我开始少考虑颜色，多考虑信息。 通过这些图像，我不断地将我所拥有的东西分为三堆。

  • 有些信息保存下来，为人所知，并且可以被描述——砖块仍然在那里，我可以在上面行走并拍摄它们。
  • 一些信息可以合理推断——20 年代的工作服颜色范围有限； 20世纪70年代的汽车可能是淡蓝色的，合理的猜测就是合理的猜测。
  • 有些信息只是丢失了，或者从一开始就不存在，这就是人工智能将做出通常不会跟踪的选择的地方。

我哥哥的眉毛和那个标志上的文字属于最后一类——至少根据现有的资料来看是这样。 如果道路工作人员的底片在某个地方幸存下来，也许可以通过滚筒扫描来提取可用信息的每个像素，然后标志就会变得清晰可辨。 或许。 我确信，考虑到制作这张照片的镜头和胶片的真正局限性，鼓式扫描我兄弟的底片不会神奇地让他的眉毛出现。

我回去明确告诉 ChatGPT 不要解析文件中不存在的详细信息。 无论如何，它仍然如此，只是程度要小得多。 它拆除了山墙上的板条并锐化了许多细节。 不过哥哥的脸色好多了。 您无需看到眉毛就能感觉到它们。 脸部追踪真实。

我不知道我需要告诉人工智能不要发明眉毛，直到我看到它发明了眉毛。 从那时起我开始了解这项技术是如何运作的：差距是需要解决的问题，而人工智能的默认就是解决它们。 除非你告诉它，人工智能不会让他们独自一人，即使这样，它有时仍然无法控制自己。

当我克服了恼怒之后，我意识到没有人走进暗室并在第一张纸上打印出完美的照片。 你打印了一张照片，仔细观察，发现了困扰你的事情。 用或多或少的光线重新打印。 烧毁天空。 躲开脸。 再试一次。 迭代是一个过程，而不是绕道而行。 我在这里写过这一点。

我母亲去世后，我收到了一些手绘的家庭照片。 有人熟练地做到了。 但这位艺术家做出的选择对我认识的人来说不太正确。 头发有点太金黄了。 眉毛有点太拱了。 调色师根据我向 ChatGPT 提供的相同信息进行工作：照片以及客户告诉他们的任何信息。 艺术家做出了他最好的推论。 有些有效，有些无效。

人工智能恢复的工作原理相同，这并不奇怪。 令我惊讶的是，人类在这个过程中的工作并不是监督人工智能。 这是为了清楚地传达确定性，同时也将不确定性重新带入。 当你知道颜色时就知道它们是什么，是的，但当你不知道时也知道它们是什么。 认识到可能被填补的差距和必须保持永久的差距之间的区别。

人工智能看到的是像素。 我知道扫描中包含和未包含的内容。 我知道房子是什么颜色的。 我知道那些砖块今天是什么样子。 我很熟悉我哥哥的脸，知道什么时候是错的。

最后一项可能是更重要的技能。
